content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 22, 2016

Candice Lee
City Attorney for City of Agoura Hills
Richards Watson Gershon
355 South Grand Avenue, 40th Floor
Los Angeles, CA 90010-3101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-160

Dear Ms. Lee:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Agoura Hills City
Councilmembers Bill Koehler and Denis Weber regarding their duties under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090. In addition, note that we are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice
(in re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete
and accurate. Moreover, if the facts underlying these these decisions should change, you should
contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Do the Councilmembers have a disqualifying conflict of interest that precludes them from
participating in the City Council's discussion regarding a lease agreement between the Los Angeles
Regional Interoperable Communication System ("LA-RICS") and the Morrison Ranch HOA for the
placement of a communications tower on HOA property?²

CONCLUSION

The Councilmembers would not have a conflict of interest that precludes them from
participating in the City Council's discussion regarding a lease agreement since there will be no
financial effect on the councilmembers.

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² We have not addressed your other questions in your letter since these were only pertinent if we found the
councilmembers had a conflict of interest.

File No. A-16-160
Page No. 2

FACTS

At the August 24th meeting, City staff intends to provide the City Council with a report
regarding a proposed lease agreement between LA-RICS and the Morrison Ranch Homeowners
Association (the "HOA").

• LA-RICS is a joint powers authority established by its member cities and other public
agencies to maintain an emergency communications system for the greater Los Angeles
region.

• The lease agreement with the HOA will allow LA-RICS to install a communications tower
on property owned by the HOA. On August 18, 2016 you stated that LA-RICS informed
city staff that there would be no payments to the HOA for use of the property, and that LA-
RICS would handle all the legal costs to put together the agreement so there would be no
costs incurred by the HOA.

• While the tower will be located within the City's borders, it is not subject to any City
approvals. The City will not be a party to the lease. However, City staff plans to provide the
City Council with information regarding the lease and proposed communications tower at
the August 24th meeting. Although staff is not requesting that the City Council take formal
action on this item, the Council conceivably might discuss the item and direct staff to write a
letter to LA-RICS, either supporting or opposing the lease and the proposed
communications tower.

• Councilmembers Koehler and Weber are both members of the HOA, and currently pay
annual dues of $580. The HOA has approximately 1,200 members.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one
or more of the public official's interests as specified in Section 87103. You ask only about effects
on the councilmembers' personal finances, thus we limit out analysis to effects on this interest.

Influencing: Note, that if the councilmembers were to have a conflict of interest they will be
prohibited from influencing the decision of another agency in their official capacity. Regulation
18704(c) provides:

"Using Official Position to Attempt to Influence a Decision. A public
official uses his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if the
or she:

"(2) Contacts or appears before any official in any other government
agency for the purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official acts or

File No. A-16-160
Page No. 3

purports to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in
making the contact."

Therefore, our analysis will necessarily focus on whether the lease decision of LA-RICS
(the decision the councilmembers will influence) will have a material financial effect on the
councilmembers' interests.

Foreseeability and Materiality.

A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved in
a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved in a decision include an interest that is a
named party in, or subject of, a government decision, and includes any governmental decision
affecting real property financial interests as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). (Regulation
18701(a).) None of the officials in question, nor their specific properties are named, or the subject
of, the LA-RICS communication tower decision.³

Regulation 18701(b) provides the standard when an official's financial interest is not
explicitly involved in a decision. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect
need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but if the financial result cannot be
expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.

With respect to materiality, Regulation 18702.5(a) provides: "A personal financial effect
means the financial effect of a governmental decision on the personal finances of a public official or
his or her immediate family. The financial effect is material if the official or the official's
immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision."
While the term "measurable" is not defined, Regulation 18702.2(b) cautions that the financial
effect of a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant.

According to your facts, the LA-RICS agreement with the HOA will result in no revenue or
costs to the HOA. Thus, there will be no foreseeable financial effect that could flow to either
councilmember as a homeowner in the HOA. Since there is no foreseeable and material financial
effect, the officials would not have a conflict of interest.

³ Regulation 18702.2(d)(4) provides that "[r]eal property in which an official has a financial interest does not
include any common area as part of the official's ownership interest in a common interest development as
defined in the Davis-Stirling Common Interest Development Act (Civil Code Sections 4000 et seq.)

File No. A-16-160
Page No. 4

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
